title: Go (Golang) Crash Course
description: Get Go installed, understand the language philosophy, and master variables, types, control flow, functions, and arrays/slices/maps. By end of day you can write real command-line pr

headings.subTitle: Key Learnings
headings.description:
Get Go installed, understand the language philosophy, and master variables, types, control flow, functions, and arrays/slices/maps. By end of day you can write real command-line programs.

Go (Golang) is a statically typed, compiled language designed at Google by Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson. Released in 2009, it was built to address pain points in large-scale software systems: slow compile times, complex dependency management, and lack of built-in concurrency.

# Download from https://go.dev/dl/ # Verify installation: $ go version go version go1.22.0 linux/amd64 # Create your first module $ mkdir hello && cd hello $ go mod init github.com/you/hello Hello, World package main import "fmt" func main () { fmt. Println ( "Hello, World!" ) } 💡 Every Go program must have a package main and a func main() . Run it with go run main.go or build a binary with go build . 1.2 Variables, Types & Constants ▶ Declaration styles var name string = "Alice" // explicit type var age = 30 // inferred type score := 99.5 // short declaration (inside func only) var x, y int = 10 , 20 // multiple in one line Basic types bool string int / int8–64 uint / uint8–64 float32 / float64 complex64/128 byte (= uint8) rune (= int32) Zero values Every type has a zero value — 0 for numerics, "" for strings, false for bools, nil for pointers/slices/maps.

Fast compilation — large codebases compile in seconds

Garbage collected — no manual memory management

Concurrency built-in — goroutines & channels as first-class citizens

headings.subTitle: Key Learnings
headings.description: Go deeper with interfaces, methods, error handling, packages, testing, and the standard library. You'll write production-quality, idiomatic Go code by end of day.

headings.subTitle: Key Learnings
headings.description:
Master Go's unique concurrency model, build real HTTP APIs, use the database/sql package, profile your programs, and understand Go internals. By end of day you can build production services.

func worker (id int ) { fmt. Printf ( "worker %d starting\n" , id) time. Sleep (time.Second) fmt. Printf ( "worker %d done\n" , id) } func main () { for i := 1 ; i <= 5 ; i++ { go worker (i) // starts goroutine } time. Sleep ( 2 * time.Second) // wait (use sync in practice) } Channels — goroutine communication // Unbuffered (synchronous) ch := make ( chan int ) go func () { ch <- 42 }() val := <-ch // Buffered (async up to capacity) bch := make ( chan string , 3 ) bch <- "first" bch <- "second" // Range over channel go func () { for i := 0 ; i < 5 ; i++ { ch <- i } close (ch) // signals end }() for v := range ch { fmt. Println (v) } // Directional channels func producer (ch chan <- int ) { ch <- 1 } func consumer (ch <- chan int ) { v := <-ch; _ = v } select statement select { case msg := <-ch1: fmt. Println ( "from ch1:" , msg) case msg := <-ch2: fmt. Println ( "from ch2:" , msg) case ch3 <- result: fmt. Println ( "sent to ch3" ) case <-time. After ( 1 * time.Second): fmt. Println ( "timeout!" ) default : fmt. Println ( "non-blocking fallthrough" ) } 💡 Go can run millions of goroutines concurrently — each starts at ~2KB of stack. The Go scheduler multiplexes them onto OS threads (GOMAXPROCS controls parallelism, defaults to CPU count). 3.2 Sync Package & Concurrency Patterns ▶ WaitGroup var wg sync. WaitGroup for i := 0 ; i < 5 ; i++ { wg. Add ( 1 ) go func (id int ) { defer wg. Done () doWork (id) }(i) } wg. Wait () // blocks until all Done() Mutex type SafeCounter struct { mu sync. Mutex v map [ string ] int } func (c * SafeCounter ) Inc (key string ) { c.mu. Lock () defer c.mu. Unlock () c.v[key]++ } // RWMutex for read-heavy workloads var rw sync. RWMutex rw. RLock () // multiple readers OK rw. RUnlock () rw. Lock () // exclusive write rw. Unlock () Worker pool pattern func workerPool (jobs <- chan int , results chan <- int , wg *sync. WaitGroup ) { defer wg. Done () for j := range jobs { results <- j * j // process job } } jobs := make ( chan int , 100 ) results := make ( chan int , 100 ) var wg sync. WaitGroup for w := 0 ; w < 5 ; w++ { // 5 workers wg. Add ( 1 ) go workerPool (jobs, results, &wg) } for j := 1 ; j <= 50 ; j++ { jobs <- j } close (jobs) wg. Wait () close (results) sync.Once & sync.Map var once sync. Once var instance * DB func GetDB () * DB { once. Do ( func () { instance = connectDB () }) return instance } // sync.Map: concurrent-safe map var cache sync. Map cache. Store ( "key" , "value" ) val, ok := cache. Load ( "key" ) 3.3 Context Package ▶ Why context? The context package carries cancellation signals, deadlines, and request-scoped values across API boundaries and goroutines. It's the standard way to propagate cancellation in Go.

Variables escape to the heap when their lifetime exceeds the function — the compiler decides (escape analysis)

The GC is tri-color concurrent mark-and-sweep — since Go 1.14 STW pauses are under 1ms

Stack grows dynamically (starts ~8KB, copies on overflow)

Avoid allocations in hot paths: reuse buffers with sync.Pool

paragraphs.title: Day 1 · Foundations
paragraphs.description:
Get Go installed, understand the language philosophy, and master variables, types, control flow, functions, and arrays/slices/maps. By end of day you can write real command-line programs.

Go (Golang) is a statically typed, compiled language designed at Google by Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson. Released in 2009, it was built to address pain points in large-scale software systems: slow compile times, complex dependency management, and lack of built-in concurrency.

# Download from https://go.dev/dl/ # Verify installation: $ go version go version go1.22.0 linux/amd64 # Create your first module $ mkdir hello && cd hello $ go mod init github.com/you/hello Hello, World package main import "fmt" func main () { fmt. Println ( "Hello, World!" ) } 💡 Every Go program must have a package main and a func main() . Run it with go run main.go or build a binary with go build . 1.2 Variables, Types & Constants ▶ Declaration styles var name string = "Alice" // explicit type var age = 30 // inferred type score := 99.5 // short declaration (inside func only) var x, y int = 10 , 20 // multiple in one line Basic types bool string int / int8–64 uint / uint8–64 float32 / float64 complex64/128 byte (= uint8) rune (= int32) Zero values Every type has a zero value — 0 for numerics, "" for strings, false for bools, nil for pointers/slices/maps.

Fast compilation — large codebases compile in seconds

Garbage collected — no manual memory management

Concurrency built-in — goroutines & channels as first-class citizens

paragraphs.title: Day 2 · Intermediate
paragraphs.description: Go deeper with interfaces, methods, error handling, packages, testing, and the standard library. You'll write production-quality, idiomatic Go code by end of day.

paragraphs.title: Day 3 · Advanced
paragraphs.description:
Master Go's unique concurrency model, build real HTTP APIs, use the database/sql package, profile your programs, and understand Go internals. By end of day you can build production services.

func worker (id int ) { fmt. Printf ( "worker %d starting\n" , id) time. Sleep (time.Second) fmt. Printf ( "worker %d done\n" , id) } func main () { for i := 1 ; i <= 5 ; i++ { go worker (i) // starts goroutine } time. Sleep ( 2 * time.Second) // wait (use sync in practice) } Channels — goroutine communication // Unbuffered (synchronous) ch := make ( chan int ) go func () { ch <- 42 }() val := <-ch // Buffered (async up to capacity) bch := make ( chan string , 3 ) bch <- "first" bch <- "second" // Range over channel go func () { for i := 0 ; i < 5 ; i++ { ch <- i } close (ch) // signals end }() for v := range ch { fmt. Println (v) } // Directional channels func producer (ch chan <- int ) { ch <- 1 } func consumer (ch <- chan int ) { v := <-ch; _ = v } select statement select { case msg := <-ch1: fmt. Println ( "from ch1:" , msg) case msg := <-ch2: fmt. Println ( "from ch2:" , msg) case ch3 <- result: fmt. Println ( "sent to ch3" ) case <-time. After ( 1 * time.Second): fmt. Println ( "timeout!" ) default : fmt. Println ( "non-blocking fallthrough" ) } 💡 Go can run millions of goroutines concurrently — each starts at ~2KB of stack. The Go scheduler multiplexes them onto OS threads (GOMAXPROCS controls parallelism, defaults to CPU count). 3.2 Sync Package & Concurrency Patterns ▶ WaitGroup var wg sync. WaitGroup for i := 0 ; i < 5 ; i++ { wg. Add ( 1 ) go func (id int ) { defer wg. Done () doWork (id) }(i) } wg. Wait () // blocks until all Done() Mutex type SafeCounter struct { mu sync. Mutex v map [ string ] int } func (c * SafeCounter ) Inc (key string ) { c.mu. Lock () defer c.mu. Unlock () c.v[key]++ } // RWMutex for read-heavy workloads var rw sync. RWMutex rw. RLock () // multiple readers OK rw. RUnlock () rw. Lock () // exclusive write rw. Unlock () Worker pool pattern func workerPool (jobs <- chan int , results chan <- int , wg *sync. WaitGroup ) { defer wg. Done () for j := range jobs { results <- j * j // process job } } jobs := make ( chan int , 100 ) results := make ( chan int , 100 ) var wg sync. Wait () close (results) sync.Once & sync.Map var once sync. Once var instance * DB func GetDB () * DB { once. Do ( func () { instance = connectDB () }) return instance } // sync.Map: concurrent-safe map var cache sync. Map cache. Store ( "key" , "value" ) val, ok := cache. Load ( "key" ) 3.3 Context Package ▶ Why context? The context package carries cancellation signals, deadlines, and request-scoped values across API boundaries and goroutines. It's the standard way to propagate cancellation in Go.

Variables escape to the heap when their lifetime exceeds the function — the compiler decides (escape analysis)

The GC is tri-color concurrent mark-and-sweep — since Go 1.14 STW pauses are under 1ms

Stack grows dynamically (starts ~8KB, copies on overflow)

Avoid allocations in hot paths: reuse buffers with sync.Pool

title: PL/SQL — Beginner to Advanced
description:
Concepts, syntax, code examples, and interview questions across every major topic. Click any section to expand.

PL/SQL (Procedural Language / Structured Query Language) is Oracle'

headings.mainTitle: PL/SQL beginner to advanced guide with concepts and interview questions
headings.subTitle: Key Learnings
headings.description:
Concepts, syntax, code examples, and interview questions across every major topic. Click any section to expand.

PL/SQL (Procedural Language / Structured Query Language) is Oracle's procedural extension to SQL. It adds programming constructs — variables, loops, conditions, and exception handling — to the declarative SQL language, allowing complex business logic to run directly in the database.

Every PL/SQL program is a block made of up to four sections:

DECLARE -- optional: variables, cursors, exceptions v_name VARCHAR2(50) := 'Oracle' ; v_count NUMBER; BEGIN -- mandatory: executable statements v_count := 10 ; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Hello, ' || v_name); EXCEPTION -- optional: error handling WHEN OTHERS THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Error: ' || SQLERRM); END ; / Key advantage: Reduces network round-trips by bundling multiple SQL calls into one server-side execution unit. Q What is PL/SQL and how does it differ from SQL? A SQL is a declarative language used to query and manipulate data — you tell the database what you want. PL/SQL is a procedural extension that lets you express how to do it using variables, conditionals, loops, and exception handlers. It integrates seamlessly with SQL and runs entirely in the Oracle database engine, reducing network overhead and improving performance for complex operations. Q What are the sections of a PL/SQL block and which are mandatory? A A PL/SQL block has four sections: DECLARE (optional — variables, constants, cursors), BEGIN (mandatory — executable statements), EXCEPTION (optional — error handling), and END (mandatory — closes the block). Only BEGIN...END is required; the rest are optional. Variables, Data Types & Constants Declaring, initializing, %TYPE, %ROWTYPE ▼ Variables are declared in the DECLARE section. PL/SQL supports scalar types, composite types, and anchor types that bind to database column definitions.

DECLARE v_salary NUMBER(10,2) := 5000.00 ; v_name VARCHAR2(100); v_hired DATE := SYSDATE; c_max_sal CONSTANT NUMBER := 100000 ; -- %TYPE anchors to a column's data type v_emp_sal employees.salary%TYPE; -- %ROWTYPE holds an entire row v_emp_row employees%ROWTYPE; BEGIN SELECT salary INTO v_emp_sal FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 101 ; SELECT * INTO v_emp_row FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 101 ; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_emp_row.last_name); END ; / Best practice: Always use %TYPE and %ROWTYPE instead of hardcoding data types. If the column definition changes, your PL/SQL automatically adapts at compile time. Q What is the difference between %TYPE and %ROWTYPE? A %TYPE anchors a variable to the data type of a single column (e.g., employees.salary%TYPE gives you a NUMBER matching that column's precision). %ROWTYPE anchors a variable to the full structure of a table row or cursor result — it creates a record with one field per column. Use %TYPE for individual column values and %ROWTYPE when you need to hold or pass an entire row. Q What is the difference between VARCHAR2 and CHAR in PL/SQL? A CHAR is a fixed-length type — it always uses the full declared length, padding with spaces. VARCHAR2 is variable-length — it only stores the actual characters. For most use cases VARCHAR2 is preferred because it avoids trailing space issues in comparisons. CHAR is only appropriate when values are genuinely fixed-length (e.g., 2-char country codes). IF / ELSIF / ELSE Conditional branching ▼ DECLARE v_score NUMBER := 75 ; v_grade VARCHAR2(2); BEGIN IF v_score >= 90 THEN v_grade := 'A' ; ELSIF v_score >= 75 THEN v_grade := 'B' ; ELSIF v_score >= 60 THEN v_grade := 'C' ; ELSE v_grade := 'F' ; END IF ; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Grade: ' || v_grade); END ; / Q What happens if a NULL value is compared in an IF condition? A Any comparison involving NULL evaluates to NULL (not TRUE or FALSE) in PL/SQL. An IF condition that evaluates to NULL is treated as FALSE and the ELSE branch executes. Always use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL to explicitly test for NULL values rather than = NULL or != NULL. Loops — LOOP, WHILE, FOR Basic, while, numeric FOR, cursor FOR ▼ -- Basic LOOP (must use EXIT) DECLARE i NUMBER := 1 ; BEGIN LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(i); i := i + 1 ; EXIT WHEN i > 5 ; END LOOP ; END ; / -- WHILE LOOP WHILE i 10 LOOP i := i + 1 ; END LOOP ; -- Numeric FOR LOOP (i is implicit, read-only) FOR i IN 1 .. 10 LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(i); END LOOP ; -- Reverse FOR FOR i IN REVERSE 1 .. 5 LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(i); -- 5,4,3,2,1 END LOOP ; Q Can you modify the loop counter variable in a FOR loop? A No. In a numeric FOR loop, the loop counter (e.g., i) is implicitly declared and is read-only within the loop body. Any attempt to assign a value to it results in a compile-time error. If you need a modifiable counter, use a WHILE loop or a LOOP...EXIT WHEN construct with an explicitly declared variable. Implicit vs Explicit Cursors SQL%ROWCOUNT, OPEN/FETCH/CLOSE ▼ A cursor is a pointer to a private SQL work area that holds the result of a query. Implicit cursors are created automatically for single-row SELECT INTO and DML statements. Explicit cursors are declared and controlled manually.

-- Implicit cursor attributes BEGIN UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * 1.1 WHERE department_id = 10 ; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(SQL%ROWCOUNT || ' rows updated' ); END ; -- Explicit cursor DECLARE CURSOR c_emp IS SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary FROM employees WHERE department_id = 20 ; v_emp c_emp%ROWTYPE; BEGIN OPEN c_emp; LOOP FETCH c_emp INTO v_emp; EXIT WHEN c_emp%NOTFOUND; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_emp.last_name); END LOOP ; CLOSE c_emp; END ; / Shortcut: Cursor FOR loop handles OPEN, FETCH, and CLOSE automatically — prefer it when you don't need manual control. FOR rec IN ( SELECT last_name, salary FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10 ) LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(rec.last_name || ' - ' || rec.salary); END LOOP ; Q What are cursor attributes and what do they return? A %FOUND — TRUE if the last FETCH returned a row. %NOTFOUND — TRUE if the last FETCH returned no row. %ROWCOUNT — number of rows fetched so far. %ISOPEN — TRUE if the cursor is currently open. For implicit cursors, these apply to the most recent SQL statement. For explicit cursors, they apply to the named cursor. Always check %NOTFOUND inside an explicit cursor loop to avoid infinite iteration. Q What happens if you forget to close an explicit cursor? A The cursor remains open, consuming memory (cursor slots) in the Oracle shared pool. If enough cursors are left open, you may hit the OPEN_CURSORS limit and receive ORA-01000: maximum open cursors exceeded. Always close cursors when done — ideally in the EXCEPTION section as well so they're closed even on error. Parameterized & REF Cursors Dynamic queries, SYS_REFCURSOR ▼ -- Parameterized cursor DECLARE CURSOR c_dept(p_dept_id NUMBER) IS SELECT last_name FROM employees WHERE department_id = p_dept_id; BEGIN FOR r IN c_dept( 10 ) LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(r.last_name); END LOOP ; END ; / -- REF CURSOR (weak type) — return from procedure CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE get_employees( p_dept IN NUMBER, p_cur OUT SYS_REFCURSOR ) AS BEGIN OPEN p_cur FOR SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department_id = p_dept; END ; / SYS_REFCURSOR is the standard way to return result sets from stored procedures to calling applications (Java, .NET, etc.). Q What is the difference between a strong and weak REF CURSOR? A A strong REF CURSOR is declared with a specific return type (RETURN employees%ROWTYPE) — it is type-safe and allows compile-time checking. A weak REF CURSOR (SYS_REFCURSOR or declared without a RETURN clause) can hold any query result but provides no compile-time type checking. Weak cursors are more flexible and commonly used to return dynamic result sets to applications. Built-in vs User-Defined Exceptions NO_DATA_FOUND, TOO_MANY_ROWS, RAISE ▼ DECLARE v_sal employees.salary%TYPE; e_too_low EXCEPTION ; -- user-defined BEGIN SELECT salary INTO v_sal FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 999 ; -- may raise NO_DATA_FOUND IF v_sal 1000 THEN RAISE e_too_low; -- raise custom exception END IF ; EXCEPTION WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Employee not found' ); WHEN TOO_MANY_ROWS THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'More than one row returned' ); WHEN e_too_low THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Salary below minimum' ); WHEN OTHERS THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Error: ' || SQLERRM); RAISE ; -- re-raise to caller END ; / Caution: WHEN OTHERS without RAISE silently swallows errors. Always log (SQLERRM / DBMS_UTILITY.FORMAT_ERROR_BACKTRACE) and re-raise in production code. Q What is RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR and when would you use it? A RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR lets you raise a custom error with a user-defined error number (between -20000 and -20999) and a descriptive message. It propagates back to the calling application just like a system error, making it ideal for enforcing business rules. Example: RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001, 'Salary cannot exceed department budget'); The calling application receives an ORA-20001 error with your custom message. Q What is PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT? A PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT associates a user-defined exception name with a specific Oracle error number, letting you trap Oracle errors by a readable name. Example: PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT(e_fk_violation, -2292); Now you can WHEN e_fk_violation THEN instead of catching it in WHEN OTHERS and inspecting SQLCODE. Stored Procedures IN, OUT, IN OUT parameters ▼ CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE update_salary( p_emp_id IN employees.employee_id%TYPE, p_pct IN NUMBER, p_new_sal OUT employees.salary%TYPE ) AS BEGIN UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * ( 1 + p_pct/ 100 ) WHERE employee_id = p_emp_id RETURNING salary INTO p_new_sal; IF SQL%ROWCOUNT = 0 THEN RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(- 20001 , 'Employee not found' ); END IF ; END update_salary; / -- Calling the procedure DECLARE v_new_sal NUMBER; BEGIN update_salary( 101 , 10 , v_new_sal); DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'New salary: ' || v_new_sal); END ; / Q What is the difference between IN, OUT, and IN OUT parameters? A IN — read-only input. The procedure cannot modify it. Default mode. OUT — write-only output. Any value passed in is not accessible inside; the procedure writes a result back to the caller. IN OUT — both: the procedure reads the incoming value and can also modify it to return a result. Use IN OUT when you need to transform a value in place. Functions vs Procedures RETURN, calling from SQL, deterministic ▼ CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_annual_salary( p_emp_id IN employees.employee_id%TYPE ) RETURN NUMBER DETERMINISTIC -- same input always gives same output AS v_sal NUMBER; BEGIN SELECT salary * 12 INTO v_sal FROM employees WHERE employee_id = p_emp_id; RETURN v_sal; EXCEPTION WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN RETURN NULL ; END ; / -- Use directly in SQL SELECT last_name, get_annual_salary(employee_id) AS annual FROM employees; DETERMINISTIC hint: Tells the optimizer it can cache function results for repeated identical inputs — useful in queries scanning large tables. Q What restrictions apply when calling a PL/SQL function from SQL? A Functions called from SQL must: (1) not modify database tables (no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) unless called from a DML statement that already has that privilege, (2) not use COMMIT or ROLLBACK, (3) not reference package variables that change between calls in ways that affect purity, and (4) take only IN parameters (no OUT or IN OUT). The PRAGMA RESTRICT_REFERENCES directive (older) or DETERMINISTIC keyword helps declare these constraints to the optimizer. DML Triggers BEFORE/AFTER, row-level, :NEW/:OLD ▼ CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_audit_salary AFTER UPDATE OF salary ON employees FOR EACH ROW WHEN (NEW.salary <> OLD.salary) BEGIN INSERT INTO salary_audit( emp_id, old_sal, new_sal, changed_by, changed_on ) VALUES ( :NEW.employee_id, :OLD.salary, :NEW.salary, USER, SYSDATE ); END ; / :NEW — the new value after DML. :OLD — the previous value. In a BEFORE trigger you can modify :NEW to change what gets written. :OLD is read-only always. Q What is a mutating table error (ORA-04091) and how do you resolve it? A ORA-04091 occurs when a row-level trigger tries to query or modify the same table that fired the trigger — the table is "mutating" (mid-DML) so Oracle prevents reading potentially inconsistent data. Solutions: (1) Use a statement-level trigger instead of row-level if you don't need :NEW/:OLD values. (2) Collect values in a package-level collection during row-level trigger execution, then process them in an AFTER STATEMENT trigger. (3) In Oracle 11g+, use compound triggers which bundle row and statement sections together cleanly. Q What is the difference between a BEFORE and AFTER trigger? A A BEFORE trigger fires before the DML operation completes — useful for validating or modifying :NEW values before they are written. An AFTER trigger fires after the operation — useful for audit logging or cascading actions where you need the confirmed final values. BEFORE triggers can cancel the operation by raising an exception; AFTER triggers cannot change what was written. Package Specification & Body Public/private, overloading, state ▼ A package groups related procedures, functions, variables, and types. The specification declares the public interface; the body contains the implementation.

Every PL/SQL program is a block made of up to four sections:

DECLARE -- optional: variables, cursors, exceptions v_name VARCHAR2(50) := 'Oracle' ; v_count NUMBER; BEGIN -- mandatory: executable statements v_count := 10 ; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Hello, ' || v_name); EXCEPTION -- optional: error handling WHEN OTHERS THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Error: ' || SQLERRM); END ; / Key advantage: Reduces network round-trips by bundling multiple SQL calls into one server-side execution unit. A CHAR is a fixed-length type — it always uses the full declared length, padding with spaces. VARCHAR2 is variable-length — it only stores the actual characters. For most use cases VARCHAR2 is preferred because it avoids trailing space issues in comparisons. CHAR is only appropriate when values are genuinely fixed-length (e.g., 2-char country codes). IF / ELSIF / ELSE Conditional branching ▼ DECLARE v_score NUMBER := 75 ; v_grade VARCHAR2(2); BEGIN IF v_score >= 90 THEN v_grade := 'A' ; ELSIF v_score >= 75 THEN v_grade := 'B' ; ELSIF v_score >= 60 THEN v_grade := 'C' ; ELSE v_grade := 'F' ; END IF ; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Grade: ' || v_grade); END ; / Q What happens if a NULL value is compared in an IF condition? A Any comparison involving NULL evaluates to NULL (not TRUE or FALSE) in PL/SQL. An IF condition that evaluates to NULL is treated as FALSE and the ELSE branch executes. Always use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL to explicitly test for NULL values rather than = NULL or != NULL. Loops — LOOP, WHILE, FOR Basic, while, numeric FOR, cursor FOR ▼ -- Basic LOOP (must use EXIT) DECLARE i NUMBER := 1 ; BEGIN LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(i); i := i + 1 ; EXIT WHEN i > 5 ; END LOOP ; END ; / -- WHILE LOOP WHILE i 10 LOOP i := i + 1 ; END LOOP ; -- Numeric FOR LOOP (i is implicit, read-only) FOR i IN 1 .. 10 LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(i); END LOOP ; -- Reverse FOR FOR i IN REVERSE 1 .. 5 LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(i); -- 5,4,3,2,1 END LOOP ; Q Can you modify the loop counter variable in a FOR loop? A No. In a numeric FOR loop, the loop counter (e.g., i) is implicitly declared and is read-only within the loop body. Any attempt to assign a value to it results in a compile-time error. If you need a modifiable counter, use a WHILE loop or a LOOP...EXIT WHEN construct with an explicitly declared variable. Implicit vs Explicit Cursors SQL%ROWCOUNT, OPEN/FETCH/CLOSE ▼ A cursor is a pointer to a private SQL work area that holds the result of a query. Implicit cursors are created automatically for single-row SELECT INTO and DML statements. Explicit cursors are declared and controlled manually.

-- Implicit cursor attributes BEGIN UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * 1.1 WHERE department_id = 10 ; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(SQL%ROWCOUNT || ' rows updated' ); END ; -- Explicit cursor DECLARE CURSOR c_emp IS SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary FROM employees WHERE department_id = 20 ; v_emp c_emp%ROWTYPE; BEGIN OPEN c_emp; LOOP FETCH c_emp INTO v_emp; EXIT WHEN c_emp%NOTFOUND; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_emp.last_name); END LOOP ; CLOSE c_emp; END ; / Shortcut: Cursor FOR loop handles OPEN, FETCH, and CLOSE automatically — prefer it when you don't need manual control. FOR rec IN ( SELECT last_name, salary FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10 ) LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(rec.last_name || ' - ' || rec.salary); END LOOP ; Q What are cursor attributes and what do they return? A %FOUND — TRUE if the last FETCH returned a row. %NOTFOUND — TRUE if the last FETCH returned no row. %ROWCOUNT — number of rows fetched so far. %ISOPEN — TRUE if the cursor is currently open. For implicit cursors, these apply to the most recent SQL statement. For explicit cursors, they apply to the named cursor. Always check %NOTFOUND inside an explicit cursor loop to avoid infinite iteration. Q What happens if you forget to close an explicit cursor? A The cursor remains open, consuming memory (cursor slots) in the Oracle shared pool. If enough cursors are left open, you may hit the OPEN_CURSORS limit and receive ORA-01000: maximum open cursors exceeded. Always close cursors when done — ideally in the EXCEPTION section as well so they're closed even on error. Parameterized & REF Cursors Dynamic queries, SYS_REFCURSOR ▼ -- Parameterized cursor DECLARE CURSOR c_dept(p_dept_id NUMBER) IS SELECT last_name FROM employees WHERE department_id = p_dept_id; BEGIN FOR r IN c_dept( 10 ) LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(r.last_name); END LOOP ; END ; / -- REF CURSOR (weak type) — return from procedure CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE get_employees( p_dept IN NUMBER, p_cur OUT SYS_REFCURSOR ) AS BEGIN OPEN p_cur FOR SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department_id = p_dept; END ; / SYS_REFCURSOR is the standard way to return result sets from stored procedures to calling applications (Java, .NET, etc.). Q What is the difference between a strong and weak REF CURSOR? A A strong REF CURSOR is declared with a specific return type (RETURN employees%ROWTYPE) — it is type-safe and allows compile-time checking. A weak REF CURSOR (SYS_REFCURSOR or declared without a RETURN clause) can hold any query result but provides no compile-time type checking. Weak cursors are more flexible and commonly used to return dynamic result sets to applications. Built-in vs User-Defined Exceptions NO_DATA_FOUND, TOO_MANY_ROWS, RAISE ▼ DECLARE v_sal employees.salary%TYPE; e_too_low EXCEPTION ; -- user-defined BEGIN SELECT salary INTO v_sal FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 999 ; -- may raise NO_DATA_FOUND IF v_sal 1000 THEN RAISE e_too_low; -- raise custom exception END IF ; EXCEPTION WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Employee not found' ); WHEN TOO_MANY_ROWS THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'More than one row returned' ); WHEN e_too_low THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Salary below minimum' ); WHEN OTHERS THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'Error: ' || SQLERRM); RAISE ; -- re-raise to caller END ; / Caution: WHEN OTHERS without RAISE silently swallows errors. Always log (SQLERRM / DBMS_UTILITY.FORMAT_ERROR_BACKTRACE) and re-raise in production code. Q What is RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR and when would you use it? A RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR lets you raise a custom error with a user-defined error number (between -20000 and -20999) and a descriptive message. It propagates back to the calling application just like a system error, making it ideal for enforcing business rules. Example: RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001, 'Salary cannot exceed department budget'); The calling application receives an ORA-20001 error with your custom message. Q What is PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT? A PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT associates a user-defined exception name with a specific Oracle error number, letting you trap Oracle errors by a readable name. Example: PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT(e_fk_violation, -2292); Now you can WHEN e_fk_violation THEN instead of catching it in WHEN OTHERS and inspecting SQLCODE. Stored Procedures IN, OUT, IN OUT parameters ▼ CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE update_salary( p_emp_id IN employees.employee_id%TYPE, p_pct IN NUMBER, p_new_sal OUT employees.salary%TYPE ) AS BEGIN UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * ( 1 + p_pct/ 100 ) WHERE employee_id = p_emp_id RETURNING salary INTO p_new_sal; IF SQL%ROWCOUNT = 0 THEN RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(- 20001 , 'Employee not found' ); END IF ; END update_salary; / -- Calling the procedure DECLARE v_new_sal NUMBER; BEGIN update_salary( 101 , 10 , v_new_sal); DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE( 'New salary: ' || v_new_sal); END ; / Q What is the difference between IN, OUT, and IN OUT parameters? A IN — read-only input. The procedure cannot modify it. Default mode. OUT — write-only output. Any value passed in is not accessible inside; the procedure writes a result back to the caller. IN OUT — both: the procedure reads the incoming value and can also modify it to return a result. Use IN OUT when you need to transform a value in place. Functions vs Procedures RETURN, calling from SQL, deterministic ▼ CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_annual_salary( p_emp_id IN employees.employee_id%TYPE ) RETURN NUMBER DETERMINISTIC -- same input always gives same output AS v_sal NUMBER; BEGIN SELECT salary * 12 INTO v_sal FROM employees WHERE employee_id = p_emp_id; RETURN v_sal; EXCEPTION WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN RETURN NULL ; END ; / -- Use directly in SQL SELECT last_name, get_annual_salary(employee_id) AS annual FROM employees; DETERMINISTIC hint: Tells the optimizer it can cache function results for repeated identical inputs — useful in queries scanning large tables. Q What restrictions apply when calling a PL/SQL function from SQL? A Functions called from SQL must: (1) not modify database tables (no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) unless called from a DML statement that already has that privilege, (2) not use COMMIT or ROLLBACK, (3) not reference package variables that change between calls in ways that affect purity, and (4) take only IN parameters (no OUT or IN OUT). The PRAGMA RESTRICT_REFERENCES directive (older) or DETERMINISTIC keyword helps declare these constraints to the optimizer. DML Triggers BEFORE/AFTER, row-level, :NEW/:OLD ▼ CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_audit_salary AFTER UPDATE OF salary ON employees FOR EACH ROW WHEN (NEW.salary <> OLD.salary) BEGIN INSERT INTO salary_audit( emp_id, old_sal, new_sal, changed_by, changed_on ) VALUES ( :NEW.employee_id, :OLD.salary, :NEW.salary, USER, SYSDATE ); END ; / :NEW — the new value after DML. :OLD — the previous value. In a BEFORE trigger you can modify :NEW to change what gets written. :OLD is read-only always. Q What is a mutating table error (ORA-04091) and how do you resolve it? A ORA-04091 occurs when a row-level trigger tries to query or modify the same table that fired the trigger — the table is "mutating" (mid-DML) so Oracle prevents reading potentially inconsistent data. Solutions: (1) Use a statement-level trigger instead of row-level if you don't need :NEW/:OLD values. (2) Collect values in a package-level collection during row-level trigger execution, then process them in an AFTER STATEMENT trigger. (3) In Oracle 11g+, use compound triggers which bundle row and statement sections together cleanly. Q What is the difference between a BEFORE and AFTER trigger? A A BEFORE trigger fires before the DML operation completes — useful for validating or modifying :NEW values before they are written. An AFTER trigger fires after the operation — useful for audit logging or cascading actions where you need the confirmed final values. BEFORE triggers can cancel the operation by raising an exception; AFTER triggers cannot change what was written. Package Specification & Body Public/private, overloading, state ▼ A package groups related procedures, functions, variables, and types. The specification declares the public interface; the body contains the implementation.
